tweets.text:
Jeremy wrote one of the best job descriptions I've seen and has a great way of thinking about them:

"Job descriptions are written for nobody to be read by nobody.

What's so important in a job description is disqualifying who you don't want. 

The nice thing about a divisive statement is that when it resonates, it deeply resonates.

The one that got the most reaction was 'you're an ideological minority at a top 10 school.' 

It's entirely open for interpretation. I had people select out. That's great, 'cause I don't want those people.

There's some schools that are uncontroversially top 10, and so I get those people. 

But you can also just assert that your school is a top 10. 

I had a few people from schools that I would not say were anywhere close just assert that. I thought that was great."
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
My second conversation with @jeremygiffon. His first episode became one of the most popular we've ever done.

Since then he's become a friend I talk to every day, so this is a taste of one of those conversations.

We discuss:
- The billion dollar PDF
- Why billionaires have https://t.co/oM4FJpy1hH

tweets.text:
it is accretive fren, the cash from the private shares go into the AUM which is then used for investments. That money isn't taken out, it's used to invest into new companies, or new rounds at higher vals. It also adds value to the shares of the existing holders. 

This likely raised the NAV based on my math from 10.51 to 10.97 per share. So a .46c gain. Which causes compression. The people who bought in the private issuance paid 3.38x nav. If you look at the chart, it always bottoms around the new 3x nav which is always higher each time. 

The bigger the premium they are issued at, the bigger the gift to exiting share holders. But the biggest value and bet is that you see the existing portco's do new rounds in the future. Which is the whole bet. 1x nav means you are buying liquid shares of private round companies at valuations that NVDA got in which isnt going to happen.

if for examples apptronik or figure ai do a new round at lets say 2x the last round. then you get all of that upside.
tweets.full_text:
it is accretive fren, the cash from the private shares go into the AUM which is then used for investments. That money isn't taken out, it's used to invest into new companies, or new rounds at higher vals. It also adds value to the shares of the existing holders. 

This likely raised the NAV based on my math from 10.51 to 10.97 per share. So a .46c gain. Which causes compression. The people who bought in the private issuance paid 3.38x nav. If you look at the chart, it always bottoms around the new 3x nav which is always higher each time. 

The bigger the premium they are issued at, the bigger the gift to exiting share holders. But the biggest value and bet is that you see the existing portco's do new rounds in the future. Which is the whole bet. 1x nav means you are buying liquid shares of private round companies at valuations that NVDA got in which isnt going to happen.

if for examples apptronik or figure ai do a new round at lets say 2x the last round. then you get all of that upside.

tweets.text:
Today @AdamWeitsman joins the Yuga Labs Board of Directors.

Building toward the next cycle means putting the right people in the right seats.

Adam is a self-made entrepreneur across multiple industries and one of the most impactful collectors in the space. But what sets him apart is character - he’s been a genuine friend to me and to this community when it counted most.

If you’ve ever spent time with Adam, you’d know that he’s got an incredible work ethic, relentlessly pursuing success in everything he does.

He’s the kind of person you want in your corner heading into the next cycle.

BAYC, Otherside, and ApeCoin.

The trenches feel pretty dead to me. The few who are left seem desperate for a quick win, no matter how small it might be.

I call that progress.

tweets.text:
Whenever I feel down — whether it’s bc of a bad trade, a market crash, a failed business, taxes, life, etc — I remember this quote by Cicero

“If you have a garden and a library, you have everything you need”

And life starts to feel a bit better

And I don’t even have a garden

tweets.text:
Competition from Robinhood seems to have lit a fire under them, and these changes are long overdue.

It was frustrating watching Base waste its massive distribution advantage by trying to force creator coins and SocialFi when no one cared. 

They should’ve pushed Crypto x AI when Virtuals and Bankr emerged organically. Tokenized assets were another obvious opportunity they left open.

They get a 2nd chance now with Cobie leading the App.

Let’s see if they execute.

Regardless, respect to Jesse. Can't have been easy by any means leading the chain the past few days.

Overall, I'm bullish on Base

tweets.full_text:
Competition from Robinhood seems to have lit a fire under them, and these changes are long overdue.

It was frustrating watching Base waste its massive distribution advantage by trying to force creator coins and SocialFi when no one cared. 

They should’ve pushed Crypto x AI when Virtuals and Bankr emerged organically. Tokenized assets were another obvious opportunity they left open.

They get a 2nd chance now with Cobie leading the App.

Let’s see if they execute.

Regardless, respect to Jesse. Can't have been easy by any means leading the chain the past few days.

Overall, I'm bullish on Base

tweets.text:
$AAPL closed up 4% vs the Nasdaq  up 0.5%.

The new Siri AI in iOS 27 public beta released yesterday gives investors concrete evidence that Apple finally has the AI chops it’s long been missing. This is the same setup that fueled Google’s sharp rerating in April 2025. 

After using the beta for a day, I can attest it’s like having a new phone .

tweets.text:
The college GPA is dead. 

My eyes have been opened to how AI is reshaping college learning. Based on a conversation today with 5 Deepwater interns (all rising juniors), here are 7 takeaways.

Bottom line: Employers hiring college grads should flat out ignore the GPA. Actually, they should hire the low GPA student because they likely invested more into strengthening their critical thinking. 

1. College isn’t really about learning anymore. AI frees up time for life experiences, friends, working out, the pool, partying, and extracurriculars. It’s about the fun and the experience.

2. The prevailing mindset is simple: You need a degree to get the job, so the goal is getting the degree as easy as possible, maximize the fun, and figure out the rest later.

3. The entire system is built around grades. GPA is everything, and students will do whatever it takes to protect it.

4. Even students who were initially morally opposed to using AI eventually give in, they have to stay competitive with everyone else who is.

5. It doesn’t seem to bother them that most are completely indistinguishable from one another. No one stands out. The reason is grades matter.

6. The majority of their homework, all writing, math, accounting, reading reflections, and summaries, is completed with AI.

7. Cheating on tests in class room is widespread. While most tests are still in-class, on paper, and multiple choice, students in bigger lecture halls (50+ people) quietly pull up an LLM on their phone and snap a photo of the question whenever the professor isn’t looking.
tweets.full_text:
The college GPA is dead. 

My eyes have been opened to how AI is reshaping college learning. Based on a conversation today with 5 Deepwater interns (all rising juniors), here are 7 takeaways.

Bottom line: Employers hiring college grads should flat out ignore the GPA. Actually, they should hire the low GPA student because they likely invested more into strengthening their critical thinking. 

1. College isn’t really about learning anymore. AI frees up time for life experiences, friends, working out, the pool, partying, and extracurriculars. It’s about the fun and the experience.

2. The prevailing mindset is simple: You need a degree to get the job, so the goal is getting the degree as easy as possible, maximize the fun, and figure out the rest later.

3. The entire system is built around grades. GPA is everything, and students will do whatever it takes to protect it.

4. Even students who were initially morally opposed to using AI eventually give in, they have to stay competitive with everyone else who is.

5. It doesn’t seem to bother them that most are completely indistinguishable from one another. No one stands out. The reason is grades matter.

6. The majority of their homework, all writing, math, accounting, reading reflections, and summaries, is completed with AI.

7. Cheating on tests in class room is widespread. While most tests are still in-class, on paper, and multiple choice, students in bigger lecture halls (50+ people) quietly pull up an LLM on their phone and snap a photo of the question whenever the professor isn’t looking.

tweets.text:
Everything in the economy runs on the same input. Compute, AI, robotics, manufacturing, transport... it's all energy underneath.

That matters now because of demographics. Fewer workers every year means the economy only grows if output per head grows fast, and the engine for that is the Exponential Age... AI and robotics doing the work people used to.

But all of that runs on energy. So the cost of energy is the ceiling on the whole thing. 

That's why the EU, Japan, China, the US and the UK are all moving at the same time.

If we can take the cost of energy to near zero, GDP per capita stops being hostage to the price of a barrel of oil.

That's the way out of this mess.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
The biggest problem humanity faces is that we're running out of people.

Sounds insane, I know. Fewer people means fewer mouths to feed, more resources to go around, and less pressure on the planet. All true, until you follow it through.

GDP is driven by demographics. A smaller https://t.co/y9nc4pOinv

But all of that runs on energy. So the cost of energy is the ceiling on the whole thing. 

That's why the EU, Japan, China, the US and the UK are all moving at the same time.

If we can take the cost of energy to near zero, GDP per capita stops being hostage to the price of a barrel of oil.

tweets.text: Some decisions are best made after acquiring more information; some are best made immediately. Just as you need to constantly sort the big from the small when you are synthesizing what’s going on, you need to constantly evaluate the marginal benefit of gathering more information against the marginal cost of waiting to decide. #principleoftheday
